the number of pages read each day follows a quadratic sequence defined by the terms 5, 12, and 21 for days 1, 2, and 3 respectively. to find the general formula for the number of pages \( P(n) \) read on the \( n \)-th day, we assume the standard form of a quadratic equation \( P(n) = an^2 + bn + c \). substituting the known values into this equation creates a system of three linear equations. for \( n = 1 \), the equation becomes \( a(1)^2 + b(1) + c = 5 \), which simplifies to \( a + b + c = 5 \). for \( n = 2 \), the equation becomes \( a(2)^2 + b(2) + c = 12 \), which simplifies to \( 4a + 2b + c = 12 \). for \( n = 3 \), the equation becomes \( a(3)^2 + b(3) + c = 21 \), which simplifies to \( 9a + 3b + c = 21 \).

to solve this system, we first subtract the first equation from the second equation to eliminate \( c \). subtracting \( a + b + c = 5 \) from \( 4a + 2b + c = 12 \) yields \( 3a + b = 7 \). next, we subtract the second equation from the third equation to eliminate \( c \) again. subtracting \( 4a + 2b + c = 12 \) from \( 9a + 3b + c = 21 \) yields \( 5a + b = 9 \). now we have a new system of two equations with two variables: \( 3a + b = 7 \) and \( 5a + b = 9 \). subtracting the first of these new equations from the second gives \( 2a = 2 \), which means \( a = 1 \). substituting \( a = 1 \) back into \( 3a + b = 7 \) results in \( 3(1) + b = 7 \), so \( b = 4 \). finally, substituting \( a = 1 \) and \( b = 4 \) into the original equation
